message.content:
Differentiator titles and descriptions:
1. Affectionate Prosociality: Pronounced desire to hug, touch, and emotionally support others, emphasizing unconditional friendship over sexual motives. In the HIGH group, participants consistently express strong interpersonal warmth and a physical-affection drive—wanting to hug, touch, listen, and actively support others. They narrate prioritizing “unconditional friendship,” describe intimate relational experiences in detail, and frame interactions around empathy and bonding. In contrast, LOW group participants often discuss sociability more analytically (e.g., “talkative” vs. “receiving”), compartmentalize emotions, and report minimal changes in warmth; they may note boundaries (e.g., hesitating to hug staff) without the pronounced impulse toward tactile, prosocial connection seen in the HIGH group.

2. Dreamlike Sensory Amplification: Dreamlike sensory amplification—brighter colors, foggy vision, dilated pupils, and pleasant tactile sensitivity are salient. HIGH group transcripts repeatedly describe vivid sensory and perceptual shifts: brighter colors, foggy or dreamlike visual quality while remaining oriented, conspicuously dilated pupils, and enhanced, pleasant skin sensations; occasional somatic markers (jaw clench, slight shakiness) are noted. LOW group participants typically reject such changes under MDMA, emphasizing reality-testing and clarity (“things are quite real”), downplaying color/vision shifts (often attributing blurring to LSD), and describing subtler bodily sensations (e.g., lightness in legs) without the pronounced dreamlike quality.

3. Procedural Vigilance and Boundary Sensitivity: High procedural vigilance and boundary sensitivity—strong self-monitoring of performance and discomfort with professional distancing in the study setting. HIGH group participants are markedly preoccupied with task performance, potential confounds, and study design (e.g., caffeine restrictions, placebo effects, environment mismatch), and they actively push themselves to perform at “100%,” monitor memory lapses, and worry about drowsiness confounding results. They also express discomfort with professional boundaries, wondering whether staff are “genuine” and lamenting the loss of “buddy-buddy” connection. LOW group participants do ask procedural questions, but these are more detached and curiosity-driven; they tend to accept the setting, focus on emotional processing, and show less performance-driven vigilance or boundary distress.

Differentiator variable names:
differentiator_01_name: prosocial_affection, differentiator_02_name: dreamlike_sensory_amplification, differentiator_03_name: boundary_procedural_vigilance
